name: Glutamate and glutamine metabolism
summary: The nitrogen-trafficking hub of the cell: interconversion of glutamate, glutamine and 2-oxoglutarate that assimilates and disposes of ammonia and feeds carbon skeletons to the TCA cycle.
description: Glutamate and glutamine sit at the crossroads of carbon and nitrogen metabolism. Glutamate is the central amino-group donor/acceptor (through transaminases and glutamate dehydrogenase) and is directly interconvertible with the TCA-cycle intermediate 2-oxoglutarate (α-ketoglutarate), making this module the principal gateway for anaplerosis and cataplerosis. Glutamine is the body's most abundant free amino acid and the main circulating nitrogen carrier: glutamine synthetase (GLUL) fixes free ammonia onto glutamate at the cost of ATP, while glutaminase (GLS/GLS2) releases it again. Downstream, glutamate is the obligate precursor of the inhibitory neurotransmitter GABA (glutamate decarboxylase), of glutathione (glutamate–cysteine ligase), of N-acetylglutamate (the essential allosteric activator of the urea cycle), of proline and ornithine, and the amide nitrogen of glutamine donates to purine, pyrimidine and hexosamine biosynthesis. Because proliferating and tumour cells are frequently 'glutamine-addicted' — routing glutamine through glutaminolysis to 2-oxoglutarate for energy and biosynthesis — glutaminase is an actively pursued oncology drug target.

references.title: Glutathione metabolism and its implications for health
The role of glutaminase in cancer
Therapeutic Targeting of Glutaminolysis as a Novel Strategy to Combat Cancer Stem Cells

compartments.name: Cytosol
compartments.description: Site of glutamine synthetase, alanine aminotransferase, glutamate decarboxylase, glutamate–cysteine ligase and GFPT1.

compartments.name: Mitochondrial matrix
compartments.description: Site of glutaminase, glutamate dehydrogenase, mitochondrial aspartate aminotransferase and N-acetylglutamate synthase.

metabolites.name: L-Glutamate
metabolites.description: Central nitrogen hub; excitatory neurotransmitter and amino-group donor. Predominant species at physiological pH is the (1-) anion.

metabolites.name: L-Glutamine
metabolites.description: Most abundant free amino acid; principal circulating nitrogen carrier and a major respiratory fuel for gut, immune and tumour cells.

metabolites.name: 2-Oxoglutarate (α-ketoglutarate)
metabolites.description: TCA-cycle intermediate and carbon skeleton of glutamate; cosubstrate of 2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases.

metabolites.name: Ammonia
metabolites.description: Free ammonia; at physiological pH exists predominantly as ammonium (NH4+). Toxic, especially to the CNS; assimilated by GLUL/GLUD1 and released by GLS.

metabolites.name: L-Aspartate
metabolites.description: Transamination partner of glutamate; donates the second nitrogen of urea and nitrogen to purine/pyrimidine synthesis.

metabolites.name: Oxaloacetate
metabolites.description: TCA-cycle intermediate; amino-group acceptor in the aspartate aminotransferase reaction and node of the malate–aspartate shuttle.

metabolites.name: L-Alanine
metabolites.description: Amino-group carrier in the glucose–alanine (Cahill) cycle between muscle and liver.

metabolites.name: Pyruvate
metabolites.description: Amino-group acceptor of alanine aminotransferase; links this module to glycolysis and gluconeogenesis.

metabolites.name: γ-Aminobutyrate (GABA)
metabolites.description: Principal inhibitory neurotransmitter of the CNS, made from glutamate by glutamate decarboxylase.

metabolites.name: N-Acetyl-L-glutamate
metabolites.description: Obligate allosteric activator of carbamoyl-phosphate synthetase 1 — the metabolic switch that turns on the urea cycle.

metabolites.name: D-Glucosamine 6-phosphate
metabolites.description: First committed intermediate of the hexosamine biosynthetic pathway, leading to UDP-GlcNAc.

metabolites.name: L-Cysteine
metabolites.description: Rate-limiting substrate for glutathione synthesis; condenses with glutamate at the γ-carboxyl.

metabolites.name: γ-L-Glutamyl-L-cysteine
metabolites.description: Committed precursor of glutathione, formed by the ATP-dependent glutamate–cysteine ligase.

metabolites.name: D-Fructose 6-phosphate
metabolites.description: Glycolytic intermediate; amino-acceptor for GFPT1, coupling glycolysis to hexosamine biosynthesis.

metabolites.name: GTP
metabolites.description: Signals a replete TCA cycle / high energy charge; allosteric inhibitor of glutamate dehydrogenase.

metabolites.name: L-Leucine
metabolites.description: Branched-chain amino acid; allosteric activator of glutamate dehydrogenase, coupling amino-acid abundance to anaplerosis and insulin secretion.

metabolites.name: L-Arginine
metabolites.description: Essential allosteric activator of N-acetylglutamate synthase; couples amino-acid load to urea-cycle activation.

metabolites.name: Glutathione (reduced, GSH)
metabolites.description: Principal cellular antioxidant and redox buffer; feedback-inhibits its own synthesis at glutamate–cysteine ligase.

metabolites.name: UDP-N-acetyl-α-D-glucosamine (UDP-GlcNAc)
metabolites.description: End product of the hexosamine biosynthetic pathway and substrate for O-GlcNAcylation; feedback-inhibits GFPT1.

enzymes.name: Glutamine synthetase
enzymes.description: Ligates ammonia to glutamate to form glutamine; the sole route for net ammonia fixation into glutamine in mammals.

enzymes.name: Glutaminase, kidney-type (GLS / KGA / GAC)
enzymes.description: Phosphate-activated mitochondrial glutaminase; the entry enzyme of glutaminolysis and a prominent cancer drug target.

enzymes.name: Glutaminase, liver-type (GLS2 / LGA / GAB)
enzymes.description: Liver-predominant glutaminase isozyme; a p53 target with context-dependent tumour-suppressive roles.

enzymes.name: Glutamate dehydrogenase 1, mitochondrial
enzymes.description: Dual-cofactor [NAD(P)+] enzyme interconverting glutamate and 2-oxoglutarate + ammonia; allosterically controlled by ADP, GTP and leucine.

enzymes.name: Aspartate aminotransferase, mitochondrial (mAST / GOT2)
enzymes.description: PLP-dependent transaminase interconverting aspartate/2-oxoglutarate and oxaloacetate/glutamate; component of the malate–aspartate shuttle.

enzymes.name: Aspartate aminotransferase, cytoplasmic (cAST / GOT1)
enzymes.description: Cytosolic isozyme of aspartate aminotransferase; cooperates with GOT2 in the malate–aspartate shuttle.

enzymes.name: Alanine aminotransferase 1 (ALT1 / GPT)
enzymes.description: PLP-dependent transaminase of the glucose–alanine cycle; serum ALT is a clinical marker of hepatocellular injury.

enzymes.name: Glutamate decarboxylase 2 (GAD65)
enzymes.description: Synaptic-vesicle-associated GABA-synthesising enzyme; a major autoantigen in type 1 diabetes and stiff-person syndrome.

enzymes.name: Glutamate decarboxylase 1 (GAD67)
enzymes.description: Constitutively active cytosolic GABA-synthesising isozyme supplying the bulk of basal neuronal GABA.

enzymes.name: N-acetylglutamate synthase, mitochondrial
enzymes.description: Produces N-acetylglutamate, the obligate activator of the urea cycle; arginine-activated. NAGS deficiency causes hyperammonaemia.

enzymes.name: Glutamate–cysteine ligase, catalytic subunit
enzymes.description: Catalytic (heavy) subunit that performs the rate-limiting, feedback-controlled first step of glutathione biosynthesis.

enzymes.name: Glutamine–fructose-6-phosphate aminotransferase 1 (GFAT1)
enzymes.description: Rate-limiting enzyme of the hexosamine biosynthetic pathway; transfers the glutamine amide to fructose-6-phosphate.

reactions.name: Glutamine synthetase (ammonia fixation)
reactions.equation: L-Glutamate + NH3 + ATP -> L-Glutamine + ADP + Pi

reactions.name: Glutamate dehydrogenase (oxidative deamination / reductive amination)
reactions.equation: L-Glutamate + H2O + NAD+ <=> 2-Oxoglutarate + NH3 + NADH + H+

reactions.name: Glutamate–cysteine ligase (γ-glutamylcysteine synthesis)
reactions.equation: L-Glutamate + L-Cysteine + ATP -> γ-L-Glutamyl-L-cysteine + ADP + Pi

regulations.mechanism: ADP (low energy charge) is a classic allosteric activator of glutamate dehydrogenase, promoting oxidative deamination and anaplerosis.
L-Leucine is an allosteric activator of GDH; gain-of-function GLUD1 variants that resist GTP inhibition cause the hyperinsulinism–hyperammonaemia syndrome.
GTP (signalling a replete TCA cycle / high energy charge) is the principal allosteric inhibitor of glutamate dehydrogenase.
Inorganic phosphate promotes assembly of the active oligomer of kidney-type (phosphate-activated) glutaminase, accelerating glutaminolysis.
Product (glutamate) inhibition of glutaminase provides local negative feedback on glutamine hydrolysis.
L-Arginine is the physiological allosteric activator of N-acetylglutamate synthase, linking a high amino-acid (nitrogen) load to production of the urea-cycle activator N-acetylglutamate.
Glutathione non-allosterically feedback-inhibits glutamate–cysteine ligase (competitive with glutamate), keeping GSH synthesis matched to demand.
UDP-GlcNAc, the end product of the hexosamine biosynthetic pathway, feedback-inhibits the rate-limiting enzyme GFPT1.
In mammals, high glutamine destabilises glutamine synthetase and accelerates its proteasomal degradation, a product-driven negative feedback distinct from the bacterial adenylylation cascade.

relations.note: 2-Oxoglutarate is a TCA-cycle intermediate; glutamate/glutamine are its principal anaplerotic source (glutaminolysis) and cataplerotic sink.
Aspartate aminotransferase couples glutamate/2-oxoglutarate to oxaloacetate, the entry point of the TCA cycle and of the malate–aspartate shuttle.
Alanine aminotransferase links glutamate metabolism to pyruvate in the glucose–alanine cycle and gluconeogenesis.
GFPT1 siphons fructose-6-phosphate out of glycolysis into hexosamine biosynthesis, using the glutamine amide nitrogen.
N-Acetylglutamate is the obligate allosteric activator of carbamoyl-phosphate synthetase 1, switching on the urea cycle.
Transaminase-generated aspartate donates the second nitrogen atom of urea via argininosuccinate synthesis.
γ-Glutamylcysteine from GCLC is the committed precursor that glutathione synthetase converts to glutathione.
The glutamine amide nitrogen is donated (via amidophosphoribosyltransferase, PPAT) to initiate de novo purine biosynthesis.
Glutamine supplies the amide nitrogen for carbamoyl phosphate (CAD / CPS II) in de novo pyrimidine biosynthesis.
Glucosamine-6-phosphate feeds the hexosamine pathway to UDP-GlcNAc and protein O-GlcNAcylation.
Glutamate is reduced (via ALDH18A1/P5CS) to glutamate-5-semialdehyde/Δ1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate, the branch point to proline and ornithine.
GABA produced here is the substrate of the GABA shunt and of inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission.

Many tumours are 'glutamine-addicted', routing glutamine through GLS-driven glutaminolysis to 2-oxoglutarate for anaplerosis, redox balance and biosynthesis; glutaminase inhibitors are in clinical development.
Cytosolic (GOT1) and mitochondrial (GOT2) aspartate aminotransferase isozymes cooperate across the malate–aspartate shuttle.
GAD67 (GAD1) provides basal cytosolic GABA while GAD65 (GAD2) supplies phasic, synaptic-vesicle-associated GABA synthesis.
Liver-type (GLS2) and kidney-type (GLS) glutaminases are paralogous isozymes with distinct tissue distribution and regulation.
